name: cc-skill-frontend-patterns
description: Frontend development patterns for React, Next.js, state management, performance optimization, and UI best practices.
capabilities: Implement compound component patterns for flexible UI
Create custom hooks for reusable stateful logic
Apply context and reducer pattern for complex state
Optimize performance with memoization and code splitting
author: affaan-m
securitySummary: This is a legitimate frontend development documentation skill. All 54 static findings are false positives triggered by code examples in markdown documentation. The skill teaches standard React patterns including composition, hooks, state management, and performance optimization. No actual security risks present.
valueStatement: Build maintainable React applications using proven patterns for component composition, state management, and performance optimization.
limitations: Does not include backend or API implementation
Does not provide runtime code execution

useCases.title: Build Reusable Component Libraries
useCases.description: Create flexible, reusable component APIs using compound components and composition patterns.

useCases.targetUser: React developers handling complex UI state
useCases.title: Manage Complex Application State
useCases.description: Implement scalable state management with React Context and useReducer.

useCases.title: Optimize React Application Performance
useCases.description: Apply memoization, lazy loading, and virtualization for fast user interfaces.

promptTemplates.title: Create Compound Components
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a compound component pattern for a [component name] in React. Include [sub-component-1], [sub-component-2], and [sub-component-3] that share state via context.

promptTemplates.title: Implement Custom Hook
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a custom React hook called use[Feature] that handles [specific behavior like data fetching, form validation, or local storage].

promptTemplates.title: Optimize Performance
promptTemplates.prompt: Review this React component and apply performance optimizations including useMemo, useCallback, and React.memo where appropriate.

promptTemplates.title: Add Form Validation
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a controlled form component with validation for fields: [list fields]. Include error messages and submission handling.

bestPractices: Prefer composition over inheritance for component reuse
Keep custom hooks focused on single responsibilities
Memoize expensive computations and callback functions
Use TypeScript for better component contracts
antiPatterns: Avoid updating state during render which causes infinite loops
Do not mutate objects directly; use immutable updates
Avoid passing new object references as props without memoization
Do not forget to include all dependencies in useEffect dependency arrays

faq.question: What is the difference between useMemo and useCallback?
faq.answer: useMemo memoizes a computed value, while useCallback memoizes a function. Use useMemo for expensive calculations and useCallback when passing callbacks to optimized child components.

faq.question: When should I use useReducer instead of useState?
faq.answer: Use useReducer when you have complex state logic with multiple sub-values, or when the next state depends on the previous one. It provides more predictable state transitions.

faq.question: How do compound components work?
faq.answer: Compound components use React Context to share implicit state between related components. The parent manages state and provides it via Context, allowing children to access and modify it.

faq.question: What is code splitting and why is it important?
faq.answer: Code splitting splits your bundle into smaller chunks that load on demand. It improves initial load time by only loading code needed for the current view.

faq.question: How do I prevent unnecessary re-renders?
faq.answer: Use React.memo for pure components, useMemo for expensive calculations, useCallback for function props, and ensure state updates are stable references.

faq.question: Can this skill help with Next.js development?
faq.answer: Yes, the patterns apply to Next.js including server components, client components, and the App Router. The state management and performance patterns work in both React and Next.js.
